issue.title: W25: controlled intelligence is becoming operating infrastructure.
issue.theme: AI, evidence, and regulatory systems move into controlled work
issue.dek: Nine source-backed signals across AI-device change control, QMSR enforcement, EMA data infrastructure, real-world evidence, registry governance, EU HTA planning, supply resilience, payer APIs, and platform evidence reuse.
issue.lede: This week is less about one big headline and more about the operating layer forming underneath healthcare intelligence. FDA, EMA, CMS and EU HTA sources all point in the same direction: AI, evidence, quality, access and supply decisions now need traceable records, not just strategy language.

workflow.notes: W25 selected 9 aligned to the iFeed Master Platform Signal Cockpit on 2026-06-20. DARWIN EU source routed to stable EMA DARWIN page because the captured factsheet URL redirects. Claims retain source boundaries.

signals.title: FDA puts ML radiology software with PCCP into a Class II pathway.
signals.cardFact: FDA classified radiological machine-learning quantitative imaging software with a predetermined change control plan into Class II with special controls, effective 17 June 2026.
signals.whyItMatters: For AI-device teams, change control is no longer an abstract lifecycle principle; it is becoming part of the regulated device category and evidence package.
signals.sourceTitle: Medical Devices; Radiology Devices; Classification of the Radiological Machine Learning-Based Quantitative Imaging Software With Predetermined Change Control Plan
signals.expandedSummary:
FDA’s Federal Register notice is the cleanest W25 AI-device governance signal. It creates a Class II route for a radiological ML-based quantitative imaging device category when the software includes a predetermined change control plan. The practical story is not that every AI device now has a free update path. It is that lifecycle change, verification, labelling, performance limits, and change boundaries are becoming part of the regulated evidence record.

For operators, this turns PCCP from a guidance concept into a design-control conversation: what can change, what evidence supports the change, what remains locked, and how the user and regulator can understand the boundary.

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: PCCP evidence becomes part of AI-device lifecycle planning.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: Regulatory, software quality, clinical evaluation, product, and postmarket teams.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: Map each adaptive function to planned change, verification evidence, labelling, and review owner.

visual.title: FDA puts ML radiology software with PCCP into a Class II pathway.
visual.footer: iFeed interpretation stays inside the source boundary.

signals.claimBoundary: This is a device-category classification and special-controls signal, not approval of all adaptive AI devices or a blanket acceptance of PCCPs.

signals.title: FDA warning-letter language is already bridging old QS findings into QMSR corrections.
signals.cardFact: FDA’s 15 June warning letter to Happiest Baby states that corrective actions proposed or implemented after 2 February 2026 must comply with QMSR requirements, even though the inspection occurred under the former QS Regulation.
signals.whyItMatters: QMSR is now a live operating standard for remediation, not a future readiness talking point.
signals.expandedSummary:
This is the grounding quality signal for W25. While AI gets the attention, FDA enforcement is showing how legacy QS Regulation observations can become current QMSR corrective-action obligations. The signal is not just “a company received a warning letter”; it is that remediation now needs to speak the language of the current quality-system rule.

For QMS leaders, the action is immediate: open CAPA, management review, complaint, supplier, design, and production controls and ask whether responses created after the QMSR effective date are written against the current rule and supported by objective evidence.

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: Remediation now has to meet the current QMSR operating expectation.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: QA, regulatory compliance, CAPA owners, management review, and site quality.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: Review open corrective actions and update evidence language against QMSR requirements.

visual.title: FDA warning-letter language is already bridging old QS findings into QMSR corrections.
visual.footer: iFeed interpretation stays inside the source boundary.

signals.claimBoundary: The public signal is the FDA warning-letter wording and remediation expectation; it should not be framed as a general finding about all manufacturers.

signals.title: EMA’s June board update shows regulation becoming data-connected infrastructure.
signals.cardFact: EMA’s June Management Board highlights covered CTIS workload, preparation for Biotech Act-related CTR/CTIS changes, international network collaboration, supply priorities, and a beta public API for Product Management Service data.
signals.whyItMatters: Regulatory operations are moving from document portals into connected data services, workload systems, and cross-network execution.
signals.sourceTitle: EMA Management Board highlights from June 2026 meeting
signals.expandedSummary:
This signal matters because it ties multiple infrastructure pieces together: CTIS pressure, product data APIs, Biotech Act preparation, international cooperation, and medicine-supply priorities. None of these is a standalone “news item” for operators; together they show regulatory work becoming a data and capacity system.

For regulatory intelligence teams, this changes the operating question. Tracking EMA is not only tracking guidelines. It is also tracking API availability, product-data standards, trial-system workload, and network-level priorities that shape submission planning and evidence operations.

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: EMA’s operating layer is becoming more data-connected and capacity-dependent.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: Regulatory operations, data governance, submissions, clinical operations, and portfolio teams.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: Add CTIS, PMS API, Biotech Act, and supply-priority trackers to the regulatory intelligence file.

visual.title: EMA’s June board update shows regulation becoming data-connected infrastructure.
visual.footer: iFeed interpretation stays inside the source boundary.

signals.claimBoundary: This is an infrastructure interpretation of EMA board highlights, not a claim that one board update creates a new legal obligation.

signals.title: DARWIN EU shows RWE becoming a routine regulatory capability.
signals.cardFact: EMA describes DARWIN EU as a network that delivers real-world evidence from European healthcare data to support EMA committees and national regulators across the medicinal-product lifecycle.
signals.whyItMatters: RWE is shifting from occasional supportive analysis into an organised regulatory capability with data partners, common models, study protocols and decision use.
signals.sourceTitle: Data Analysis and Real World Interrogation Network (DARWIN EU)
signals.sourceType: EMA DARWIN EU page / factsheet context
signals.expandedSummary:
The W25 value is not that RWE exists. The value is that EMA’s DARWIN EU infrastructure makes RWE repeatable: data partners, standardisation, non-interventional studies, reporting, and use by EMA committees and national regulators. That turns evidence generation into a system rather than a one-off project.

For evidence teams, this is a planning signal. If regulators can increasingly ask and answer lifecycle questions through RWE infrastructure, sponsors need stronger records for data provenance, fit-for-purpose logic, protocol rationale, uncertainty, and how RWE complements other evidence.

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: RWE is becoming a repeatable regulatory evidence service.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: RWE, epidemiology, safety, regulatory strategy, data governance, and market access.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: Create a RWE readiness record: source, population, data model, protocol, limitation, and decision use.

visual.title: DARWIN EU shows RWE becoming a routine regulatory capability.
visual.footer: iFeed interpretation stays inside the source boundary.

signals.claimBoundary: The stable EMA page supports the infrastructure reading; avoid saying the W25 page itself announced a new DARWIN obligation.

signals.title: Alzheimer registry work turns RWE governance into disease-specific evidence planning.
signals.cardFact: EMA published material from the HMA/EMA multistakeholder workshop on Alzheimer’s disease patient registries, focused on how registry data can support regulatory decision-making and long-term follow-up.
signals.whyItMatters: For complex therapies, evidence planning increasingly needs disease-specific registries, common data elements, governance, consent, quality, and interoperability before launch pressure arrives.
signals.sourceTitle: Joint HMA/EMA multistakeholder workshop on patient registries in Alzheimer’s disease
signals.expandedSummary:
This signal makes RWE concrete. Alzheimer’s disease registries are not only research infrastructure; they are becoming part of the evidence plan for long-term follow-up, safety, effectiveness, risk minimisation, and decision confidence. The operating issue is whether registry data are governed well enough to be useful.

For sponsors, registries, regulators and evidence teams, the work starts before the evidence is needed: define core data elements, consent logic, interoperability, quality assurance, patient follow-up, and how the registry will answer regulatory and access questions.

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: Disease-specific RWE needs governance before it can support decisions.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: RWE, registry owners, clinical development, safety, HEOR, regulatory and patient-engagement teams.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: Map registry readiness against data elements, consent, QA, interoperability, and decision questions.

visual.title: Alzheimer registry work turns RWE governance into disease-specific evidence planning.
visual.footer: iFeed interpretation stays inside the source boundary.

signals.claimBoundary: This is a registry-governance and evidence-planning signal, not a claim about any individual Alzheimer product outcome.

signals.title: EU HTA consultation is moving device and IVD evidence planning earlier.
signals.cardFact: The European Commission’s Joint Scientific Consultations page says JSCs support planning clinical studies or investigations for later Joint Clinical Assessment and flags a 17 June webinar for high-risk medical devices and IVD developers.
signals.whyItMatters: High-risk device and IVD teams need to align clinical evidence, regulatory strategy and HTA questions before the dossier is locked.
signals.expandedSummary:
This is a practical market-access signal. EU HTA is not just a downstream assessment step; the Joint Scientific Consultation route exists to improve evidence planning for later Joint Clinical Assessment. The W25 update matters because high-risk medical device and IVD developers are being explicitly pulled into that planning conversation.

For teams building evidence packages, the question is simple: are regulatory endpoints, clinical investigation design, comparators, patient-relevant outcomes, uncertainty and access questions being discussed early enough to survive HTA review?

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: Evidence built only for approval may not answer HTA questions.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: Market access, medtech regulatory, clinical evidence, biostatistics, HEOR and product teams.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: Add HTA comparator, outcome, uncertainty and JSC route fields to the evidence plan.

visual.title: EU HTA consultation is moving device and IVD evidence planning earlier.
visual.footer: iFeed interpretation stays inside the source boundary.

signals.claimBoundary: Do not claim a product-specific access decision; this is about the JSC planning route and public webinar/update context.

signals.title: FDA’s breast-biopsy needle notice turns supply risk into a clinical operating signal.
signals.cardFact: FDA warned healthcare providers about disruptions in breast biopsy needle availability and said supply impacts may continue through the first quarter of 2027.
signals.whyItMatters: Supply resilience is now part of clinical quality, pathway continuity, procurement planning and patient-access risk management.
signals.sourceTitle: Disruptions in Availability of Breast Biopsy Needles: Letter to Health Care Providers
signals.expandedSummary:
This is not an AI story, and that is exactly why it matters. A device supply disruption can affect screening follow-up, diagnostic workflows, procurement decisions, clinical scheduling and patient anxiety. FDA’s notice gives operators a concrete supply-risk item with an expected duration into Q1 2027.

For healthcare organisations, the signal is to move supply intelligence into pathway management: inventory visibility, clinical alternatives, prioritisation rules, communication plans, and documentation of how care continuity is protected.

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: Supply disruption can become clinical delay and access risk.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: Clinical operations, radiology/breast care, procurement, quality, risk, and patient-access teams.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: Create a disruption plan covering stock, alternatives, prioritisation, communication, and escalation.

visual.title: FDA’s breast-biopsy needle notice turns supply risk into a clinical operating signal.
visual.footer: iFeed interpretation stays inside the source boundary.

signals.claimBoundary: This source supports a supply-disruption and preparedness signal; avoid extrapolating to all biopsy devices or all care settings.

signals.title: CMS electronic prior authorization is becoming a drug-access data infrastructure issue.
signals.cardFact: The CMS-0062-P comment period closed on 15 June 2026 for a proposed rule extending FHIR-based electronic prior authorization requirements to certain drugs and payer reporting elements.
signals.whyItMatters: Prior authorization is moving from administrative friction into structured data, APIs and public performance reporting that market-access teams will need to understand.
signals.sourceTitle: CMS Interoperability Standards for Prior Authorization of Drugs Proposed Rule CMS-0062-P
signals.expandedSummary:
This signal belongs in the digest because market access is becoming digital infrastructure. CMS-0062-P is still a proposed rule process, but the close of comments moves the policy toward the next decision point. The practical relevance is that drug prior authorization may become more API-driven, more measurable, and more visible.

For pharma, payer, provider and health-IT teams, this raises an operating question: what happens when access friction becomes structured data that can be routed, measured and compared across drugs, plans and workflows?

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: Access operations may become more API-visible and benchmarkable.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: Market access, payer tech, EHR vendors, revenue cycle, policy, and patient-support teams.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: Track final-rule timing, covered drug scope, FHIR API readiness, and denial/approval reporting implications.

visual.title: CMS electronic prior authorization is becoming a drug-access data infrastructure issue.
visual.footer: iFeed interpretation stays inside the source boundary.

signals.claimBoundary: This is a proposed-rule process signal. Do not state final requirements or implementation dates as settled unless CMS finalises them.

signals.title: EMA’s platform-approach work pushes reusable evidence beyond manufacturing.
signals.cardFact: EMA published material from its platform-approaches webinar covering platform technologies, prior knowledge, platform marketing-authorisation concepts, individualised therapies, and clinical/non-clinical case studies.
signals.whyItMatters: Regulatory evidence is moving toward reusable logic: what can be carried forward, what must be product-specific, and how prior knowledge is controlled.
signals.sourceTitle: Webinar on the use of platform approaches in non-clinical and clinical domains
signals.expandedSummary:
This closes the week by connecting W25 back to a bigger iFeed theme: intelligence becomes infrastructure when evidence can be reused responsibly. EMA’s platform discussion extends the conversation beyond quality and manufacturing into non-clinical and clinical domains, where prior knowledge, individualised therapies and product-specific boundaries matter.

For development and regulatory teams, the useful question is not “can we reuse evidence?” but “which part of the evidence is platform-level, which part is product-specific, and what record proves that boundary?”

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: Platform logic can reduce repetition only when evidence boundaries are explicit.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: CMC, non-clinical, clinical, regulatory strategy, quality, statistics, and evidence planning.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: Separate platform prior knowledge, product-specific evidence, assumptions, and review ownership in one file.

visual.title: EMA’s platform-approach work pushes reusable evidence beyond manufacturing.
visual.footer: iFeed interpretation stays inside the source boundary.

signals.claimBoundary: This is an EMA platform-evidence discussion signal, not a claim that platform marketing authorisation is available for every therapy type.
